angles.label: time-scale modification algorithms
angles.query: WSOLA PSOLA phase vocoder time-scale modification speech speed-up algorithm comparison intelligibility
angles.rationale: Core DSP techniques that compress duration while preserving pitch, the basis of speed-listening playback.

angles.label: perceptual limits of accelerated speech
angles.query: perceptual limits comprehension time-compressed speech maximum word rate intelligibility threshold study
angles.rationale: Peer-reviewed research on where comprehension breaks down, around 2.5-3x or 275-400 wpm.

angles.label: training and adaptation in blind listeners
angles.query: blind listeners ultra-fast speech screen reader adaptation training 16x time-compressed comprehension neuroplasticity
angles.rationale: The above-16x claim comes from blind screen-reader users; finds studies on training and cortical adaptation.

angles.label: intelligibility enhancement and silence compression
angles.query: speech intelligibility enhancement silence pause removal spectral prosody processing smart speed dynamic compression
angles.rationale: Comprehensibility methods like silence trimming and prosody shaping, distinct from raw time-stretching.

result.summary: Strategy: split into signal-processing techniques that make audio more comprehensible and time-compressible, and the perception/adaptation science behind extreme speed listening. Angles cover TSM algorithms, the comprehension ceiling, training effects in blind screen-reader users, silence/prosody enhancement, and implementation libraries.
result.question: Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x - the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.

results.title: Time-compressed speech (Wikipedia)
results.snippet: Best single overview of perceptual limits. Establishes the key benchmarks: normal conversational English ~150 wpm; average listeners comprehend up to 200-250 wpm 'without undue difficulty'; comprehension benefit peaks around 25% compression; blind/visually-impaired listeners reach 300-350 wpm; performance declines with age, non-native material, and extreme compression, but improves with multi-day training. Directly frames the ~250-400 wpm transition zone central to the question.

results.title: A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech (Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics)
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed primary study defining a 'speech rapidity threshold' - the maximum rate an individual understands, measured via repeated comprehensibility judgments as rate varies. Key finding: thresholds for time-compressed speech are significantly higher than for speeded speech, and threshold values correlate poorly with comprehension-test scores, meaning the method captures the intelligibility ceiling rather than higher-order comprehension. Core citation for where the perceptual limit sits.

results.title: Word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech (Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics)
results.snippet: Pinpoints the mechanism behind the breakdown: reduced comprehension of compressed messages results from decreasing word intelligibility, and speech compressed to ~30% of natural duration shows markedly reduced intelligibility. Explains why comprehension collapses past a threshold rather than degrading uniformly - directly relevant to where (around 2.5-3x / 275-400 wpm) the limit lies.

results.title: The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise (PMC)
results.snippet: Recent peer-reviewed study linking individual differences in time-compressed-speech intelligibility to auditory temporal processing (listening in modulated noise). Useful for explaining why perceptual limits vary across listeners and what auditory mechanism gates the maximum tolerable rate - supports the 'where comprehension breaks down' angle with a physiological correlate.

results.title: Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation (PMC)
results.snippet: Addresses the training/adaptation side of the limit: listeners rapidly adapt to sentences compressed to ~35% of original duration within 10-20 sentences, and longer-term perceptual learning shifts the threshold further. Shows the perceptual limit is not fixed - exposure raises it - which is essential context for whether extreme speed-ups can be made intelligible through training.

results.title: Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech
results.snippet: Literature-review summary consolidating decades of accelerated-speech findings: comprehension declines slowly until roughly 275 wpm then falls more steeply; ~400 wpm marks where processing is clearly harmed; speech becomes nearly unintelligible above ~1,200 wpm. Good single source for the specific 275/400 wpm breakpoints requested in the question.

results.title: Combining spectral and temporal modification techniques for speech intelligibility enhancement (Computer Speech & Language)
results.snippet: Directly on-angle: surveys intelligibility-boosting DSP that is distinct from raw time-stretching. Covers spectral shaping (formant sharpening, reduced spectral tilt, energy reallocation across frequency) plus temporal-domain modification, and how combining them reduces energetic masking. Notes most algorithms leave the timing of underlying speech sounds intact while redistributing energy, framing prosody/spectral processing as a separate lever from speed-up.

results.title: Speech-in-noise intelligibility improvement based on spectral shaping and dynamic range compression (SSDRC — Zorila, Kandia, Stylianou)
results.snippet: Defines the canonical SSDRC pipeline: cascade of spectral shaping (frequency domain) followed by dynamic range compression (time domain), inspired by Lombard/clear-speech production. Reports listening tests with 175 subjects showing up to ~20.5% intelligibility gain in speech-shaped noise and 12.3% in competing speech. The reference method for prosody/spectral-based comprehensibility enhancement that the original question asks about.

results.title: Introducing Trim Silence: save seconds by intelligently removing pauses (RadioPublic)
results.snippet: Concrete product description of silence/pause compression as a comprehensibility-and-efficiency method separate from time-stretching: intelligently detects and removes pauses so cadence of the host is preserved. Good real-world anchor for the 'silence trimming as distinct from raw speed' part of the question, and explains why trimming dead air lets listeners go faster without distorting voice pitch.

results.title: Playback Effects (Pocket Casts) — Trim Silence and Volume Boost
results.snippet: Documents the widely used 'Trim Silence' / Smart Speed style feature: analyzes volume and timing to shorten gaps that disrupt flow while keeping natural pauses, with adjustable aggressiveness, plus volume boost (a form of dynamic range compression) to keep quiet speech intelligible. Directly illustrates silence compression and DRC as deployed comprehensibility tools, complementing the academic SSDRC work.

results.title: Spectral and temporal manipulations of SFF envelopes for enhancement of speech intelligibility in noise
results.snippet: Research on manipulating single-frequency-filtering (SFF) envelopes — both spectral and temporal envelope reshaping — to raise intelligibility. Adds depth on prosody/envelope-level processing (transient and temporal-envelope enhancement) as a comprehensibility technique that operates on the speech structure rather than simply changing playback rate.

results.title: Real-Time Modulation Enhancement of Temporal Envelopes for Increasing Speech Intelligibility
results.snippet: Describes real-time enhancement of the temporal/amplitude modulation envelope to make speech more intelligible — relevant to making accelerated or noisy speech easier to parse. Bridges the intelligibility-enhancement angle to real-time playback contexts, where envelope and dynamic-range processing keep fast or compressed audio comprehensible.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Dietrich et al., Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013)
results.snippet: The single most directly on-point source for the 'training/adaptation' angle. Late-blind subjects underwent a structured training program in ultra-fast speech comprehension; pre/post fMRI showed training itself induced functional reorganization recruiting the central-visual system (V1, fusiform gyrus). Demonstrates causally that the >16 syl/s capacity is a learned, neuroplastic adaptation rather than an innate trait, and that comprehension improves with practice. Directly explains why blind screen-reader users reach playback rates sighted listeners cannot.

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - an fMRI study (Dietrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013)
results.snippet: Establishes the quantitative perceptual limit and the neural substrate. Blind proficient listeners comprehend speech at >16 syllables/s (up to ~22 syl/s) versus a sighted ceiling near 8 syl/s. fMRI at 8 vs 16 syl/s shows blind experts uniquely activate right V1, contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar alongside the classical left-IFG/STS language network. Provides the cortical-recruitment mechanism behind extreme accelerated-speech intelligibility and the 16x-class claim.

results.title: How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects? (Hertrich et al., Frontiers in Psychology 2013)
results.snippet: Goes beyond localization to the signal-processing/perceptual mechanism. Argues blind listeners route subcortical auditory (pulvinar) information into visual cortex to represent prosody as an 'event-trigger' channel that segments and times the fast stream, and that visual-cortex engagement confers greater resistance to backward masking - i.e. enhanced temporal resolution. Bridges the neuroscience to why prosody/timing processing is what makes extreme time-compressed speech parseable.

results.title: Software Development at 800 Words Per Minute (Dickson Tan)
results.snippet: Firsthand practitioner account from a blind screen-reader user, grounding the academic claims in real usage. Explains that synthetic voices read each word identically every time, so users 'get used to' the voice; comprehension that initially requires conscious effort becomes automatic after years of practice. Concretely documents the 800-900 wpm regime (vs ~200 wpm normal speech) and frames it explicitly as a trained adaptation - the experiential counterpart to the cortical-reorganization studies.

results.title: On-line plasticity in spoken sentence comprehension: Adapting to time-compressed speech (Peelle & Wingfield 2005)
results.snippet: Addresses short-timescale (within-session) adaptation in the general population, complementing the long-term blind-expert literature. Shows even sighted listeners rapidly improve comprehension of time-compressed speech with brief exposure, and that adaptation generalizes across compression rates - establishing that rapid perceptual learning of accelerated speech is a baseline human capacity that blind users extend through extensive training.

results.title: Network Modeling for fMRI Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners (Dietrich/Hertrich et al., PLOS ONE 2015)
results.snippet: Effective-connectivity (dynamic causal modeling) analysis of how the recruited regions interact during ultra-fast comprehension in late-blind listeners. Adds mechanistic depth on the directional information flow (pulvinar to V1 to frontal language areas) underlying the adapted network, supporting the redistribution-of-brain-activity account of how extreme speed-ups become intelligible.

results.title: A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals (Driedger & Müller, Applied Sciences 2016)
results.snippet: The canonical, accessible survey of TSM. Explains and compares the three core families that underpin speed-listening playback: Overlap-Add (OLA), Waveform-Similarity Overlap-Add (WSOLA), TD-PSOLA, and the Phase Vocoder (PV-TSM), including how each compresses duration while preserving pitch and the artifacts (transient smearing, phasiness) that limit intelligibility at high factors. Best single starting point; also pairs with the authors' open MATLAB TSM toolbox and the PyTSMod Python library for hands-on implementation.

results.title: Time-Scale Modification of Speech Signals (overview / WSOLA foundations)
results.snippet: Directly addresses speech (not just music). Describes why WSOLA produces high-quality output, is computationally efficient and robust, supports online processing with arbitrary and time-varying scaling factors, and preserves naturalness, intelligibility, pitch and formant structure after time-scaling. Explains how WSOLA improves on plain OLA by waveform-similarity segment selection, and why it avoids the pitch-marking requirement of PSOLA — making it the practical default for real-time speed-up players.

results.title: Training of Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Induces Functional Reorganization in Late-Blind Humans (Frontiers / PMC)
results.snippet: Directly answers the 'speed listening above 16x' perceptual-limits angle. Reports that trained blind listeners comprehend accelerated speech up to ~22 syllables/s versus ~8 syl/s for sighted listeners, and that at 16 syl/s blind comprehension reached 93% while sighted stayed below 16%. Documents the training/adaptation effect and the neural reorganization (recruitment of visual cortex) that makes extreme speed-ups intelligible — the human-side counterpart to the DSP methods.

results.title: PVSOLA: A Phase Vocoder with Synchronized OverLap-Add (IRCAM, DAFx-11)
results.snippet: Shows the state of the art that hybridizes the two main TSM families: it injects WSOLA-style synchronized overlap-add into the phase vocoder to reset accumulated phase error, directly attacking the 'phasiness'/loss-of-presence artifact that degrades intelligibility of phase-vocoder time-stretching at large factors. Relevant for understanding how modern players push speed-up quality higher.

results.title: Improved Phase-Vocoder Time-Scale Modification of Audio (Laroche & Dolson)
results.snippet: Foundational phase-vocoder paper. Identifies the characteristic phase-vocoder artifact ('phasiness'/reverberation/loss of presence) that limits comprehensibility at high stretch factors, and introduces phase-locking extensions that significantly improve output quality. Essential background for the spectral-domain branch of speed-listening DSP and why naive STFT time-stretching sounds smeared.

results.title: The Overview of Segmental Durations Modification Algorithms on Speech Signal Characteristics (arXiv 2025)
results.snippet: Recent comparative evaluation benchmarking WSOLA, SOLAFS, and the phase vocoder on speech. Finds WSOLA gives the highest quality (least energy loss / power-spectral-density distortion) while SOLAFS and the phase vocoder are more computationally efficient (SOLAFS runs in under half WSOLA's time). Useful, up-to-date quantitative comparison for choosing an algorithm for fast playback, including notes on ESOLA outperforming PSOLA on perceptual quality and intelligibility.

results.title: PyTSMod — Python library for audio time-scale modification (OLA, WSOLA, TD-PSOLA, Phase Vocoder)
results.snippet: The single most directly buildable open-source implementation for a speed-listening pipeline. Pure Python 3 (NumPy/SciPy/soundfile, pip-installable, GPL-3.0) implementing all four core TSM algorithms the question names: OLA, WSOLA, TD-PSOLA, and Phase Vocoder. Critically for adaptive/variable-rate playback, the stretch factor accepts either a constant alpha OR a 2xn array of anchor points mapping input sample positions to output positions — exactly the mechanism needed to compress silence/stable vowels while lengthening formant transitions. Lets you prototype and A/B different TSM methods without writing DSP from scratch.

results.title: Rubber Band — frequency-domain time-stretching and pitch-shifting library (falkTX mirror / upstream)
results.snippet: Production-grade C++ TSM library (GPL, with commercial license). Works in the frequency domain for highest audio quality and handles transients well, so it preserves consonant clarity better than naive phase-vocoding at aggressive speedups — relevant to the 'most comprehensible' goal. Tradeoff explicitly noted in sources: high CPU load makes real-time mobile use hard, so it suits offline/server-side rendering of accelerated audio rather than on-device playback. Supports independent tempo vs. pitch control, important because raising rate without pitch correction shifts formants and hurts intelligibility.

results.title: SoundTouch — open-source WSOLA-based tempo/pitch/rate library (LGPL)
results.snippet: The realtime counterpart to Rubber Band: a compact LGPL-2.1 C++ library using WSOLA-like time-domain stretching that changes tempo, pitch, and playback rate independently. Low CPU load lets it run real-time on mobile, making it the practical choice for an interactive 'speed listening' player (this is the engine behind many podcast-speed and Android player implementations). Sources frame the Rubber Band vs SoundTouch decision as quality-vs-cost — the key tradeoff for anyone choosing a stretch backend.

results.title: librosa.effects.time_stretch — phase-vocoder time stretching in Python
results.snippet: Widely used Python entry point for fixed-rate time stretching via phase vocoding, convenient for rapid experimentation alongside librosa's analysis tools. Documentation candidly warns that phase vocoding can 'smear' transients and degrade quality — a useful cautionary data point explaining why speed-listening pipelines often prefer WSOLA/PSOLA (PyTSMod, SoundTouch) over a plain phase vocoder for intelligibility at high rates.

results.title: Enhancing speech intelligibility using variable-rate time-scale modification (US patent)
results.snippet: Describes the algorithmic strategy that makes extreme speed-ups intelligible: variable-rate WSOLA that uses syllable segmentation to choose a per-segment time-scaling factor — lengthening formant transitions while compressing dominant vowels and silence. This is the design blueprint behind PyTSMod's anchor-point API and the 'allocate extra duration to pauses, not uniform stretching' intelligibility finding, bridging the implementation tools to the perceptual goal.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension (16 syl/s) in late-blind humans
results.snippet: Grounds the 'speed listening beyond 16x' question in evidence: normal sighted listeners top out near 8 syllables/second, but trained vision-impaired listeners comprehend up to ~22 syl/s, and a ~6-month training study raised ultra-fast (16 syl/s) word-repetition accuracy to ~70%. Combined with perceptual-learning work showing adaptation to time-compressed speech after only 10–20 sentences, it sets the realistic perceptual ceilings and adaptation timeline any speed-listening tool must design around.

claims.claim: Normal-hearing listeners can perceive time-compressed speech up to roughly 11 to 16 syllables per second, defining an approximate perceptual ceiling for accelerated speech comprehension.
claims.quote: The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second.

claims.claim: The study time-compressed speech using the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add (PSOLA) algorithm, chosen because it shifts the modulation spectrum toward higher modulation frequencies as the compression ratio increases.
claims.quote: The LIST sentences were uniformly time-compressed at seven different compression ratios (CRs) with the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add algorithm (PSOLA)...The PSOLA algorithm was used as it shifts the modulation spectrum with increasing compression ratio to higher modulation frequencies.

claims.claim: Listeners who are better at perceiving fast (time-compressed) speech are also better at understanding speech in modulated noise, an effect that persists after controlling for performance in unmodulated noise (partial correlation r=0.41, p=0.008).
claims.quote: Listeners who are better at perceiving speech at high rates are better in listening to speech in modulated noise. This effect persisted even when controlling for their ability to perceive speech in unmodulated noise.

claims.claim: Increasing speech rate from 2.7 to 6.6 syllables per second reduced glimpsing by 5.3 dB in a 4-Hz amplitude-modulated masker, even though speech in quiet remained 100% intelligible at both rates, showing temporal processing limits emerge in noise before they appear in quiet.
claims.quote: An increase in speech rate from 2.7 to 6.6 syllables per second resulted in a reduction in glimpsing of 5.3 dB when listening to speech in a 4-Hz amplitude-modulated masker, even though speech in quiet was 100% intelligible at both rates.

claims.claim: Special populations can perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second, indicating that the neural processing of time-compressed speech can be altered by exposure and training (adaptation effects).
claims.quote: It is not uncommon that special populations perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second...indicating that the neural processing mechanisms of time-compressed speech can be altered by, for example, exposure and training.

claims.claim: Non-linear time-compression (the current preferred method) combines selectively removing silences, speeding up speech so the reduced silences sound proportioned, and then applying data algorithms to restore the original pitch.
claims.quote: The current preferred method of time-compression is called 'non-linear compression', which employs a combination of selectively removing silences; speeding up the speech to make the reduced silences sound normally-proportioned to the text; and finally applying various data algorithms to bring the speech back down to the proper pitch

claims.claim: Pitch-synchronous DSP techniques detect the start and end of each fundamental-pitch cycle within vowels (held a minimum of ~20 ms) and skip a fraction of those cycles to speed up speech without changing pitch.
claims.quote: Vowels are typically held a minimum of 20 milliseconds, over many cycles of the fundamental pitch. DSP systems can detect the beginning and end of each cycle and then skip over some fraction of those cycles, causing the material to be presented at a faster rate, without changing the pitch

claims.claim: Comprehension of accelerated speech peaks at around 25% compression (25% faster than normal), indicating a perceptual sweet spot beyond which comprehension declines.
claims.quote: the average person is capable of relatively easily comprehending speech delivered at higher-than-normal rates, with the peak occurring at around 25% compression (that is, 25% faster than normal)

claims.claim: Sighted listeners can comprehend speech up to 200-250 wpm (vs. ~150 wpm conversational baseline), while blind and severely visually impaired listeners reach comprehension at up to 300-350 wpm.
claims.quote: Conversational speech (in English) takes place at a rate of around 150 wpm (words per minute), but the average person is able to comprehend speech presented at rates of up to 200-250 wpm without undue difficulty

claims.claim: Comprehension of time-compressed speech can be improved through training/adaptation: non-native speakers improve with multiday training, and older blind subjects match or exceed younger sighted subjects.
claims.quote: Non-native speakers can, however, improve their comprehension level of time-compressed speech with multiday training

claims.claim: Reduced comprehension of time-compressed speech is primarily caused by decreased intelligibility of individual words, not just by the faster delivery rate per se.
claims.quote: Decreased comprehension of compressed messages is the result of decreased word intelligibility.

claims.claim: Deleting 60% of the speech signal reduces comprehension even when the temporal/time-limitation effects of compression are removed, showing that signal deletion alone degrades comprehension.
claims.quote: when 60% of the signal is deleted, comprehension is reduced even without the temporal limitations of compression

claims.claim: Words that are individually intelligible become less intelligible when presented inside the compressed time constraints of a fast message, indicating a perceptual/processing-time bottleneck under compression.
claims.quote: individually compressed words are less intelligible when presented within the limited time constraints of a compressed message

claims.claim: The research question of whether comprehension loss in time-compressed speech stems from reduced word intelligibility was the explicit focus of the study and was experimentally investigated using temporal interruption and backward masking.
claims.quote: The extent to which decreased comprehension of time-compressed messages results from decreased word intelligibility was investigated.

claims.claim: Time compression of speech in this study was performed using the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which preserves pitch while shortening duration and achieves high-quality time-scale modification of speech.
claims.quote: Time compression throughout the experiment was carried out using an implementation of the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which has been shown to achieve very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals.

claims.claim: Comprehension of time-compressed speech improves rapidly with brief exposure (10-20 sentences), but this rapid adaptation leaves performance well below ceiling, indicating brief adaptation alone is insufficient for full intelligibility at high compression.
claims.quote: Brief adaptation (10–20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40–85% correct).

claims.claim: Extended multiday training produces substantially more perceptual learning of time-compressed speech than brief single-session adaptation, showing that adaptation to accelerated speech has both a rapid phase and a slower training-dependent phase.
claims.quote: Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation.

claims.claim: Speech was compressed down to as little as 20% of its original duration (equivalent to roughly 5x speed-up), with sentences typically tested at 30-45% of original duration, and listeners could be trained to comprehend these heavily compressed signals.
claims.quote: Compression level was modified logarithmically, using a scale of 25 logarithmically equal steps between the starting value and the maximal level of compression (20%).

claims.claim: With multiday training, non-native listeners reached comprehension performance equivalent to native speakers on trained conditions, demonstrating that training can close large initial intelligibility gaps for accelerated speech.
claims.quote: Performance of the trained group was equivalent to that of the native speakers on all conditions on which learning occurred.

claims.claim: RadioPublic launched a 'Trim Silence' feature on March 11, 2019 that removes pauses from podcast audio to reduce listening time.
claims.quote: intelligently remove the pauses in your favorite shows so you can can fill your ears with even more podcasts

claims.claim: Trim Silence is a user-toggleable playback option configurable per-podcast or globally, accessed via the player's playback speed controls.
claims.quote: Tap the ^ arrow in the player, then tap the 1x playback speed controls and you can set Trim Silence for all podcasts or a specific podcast.

claims.claim: Removing pauses produces a cumulative time saving across episodes, framed as freeing up time for additional listening rather than a quantified per-episode figure.
claims.quote: Those saved seconds add up, so why not add another episode or two to your queue to make up your lost time?

claims.claim: Time-compressed speech (compressed by the sampling method) yields significantly higher speech-rate intelligibility thresholds than simple speeded speech, meaning sampling-based compression preserves intelligibility at faster rates than naive speed-up.
claims.quote: The first experiment compared thresholds of two types of rapid speech reportedly different in intelligibility: simple speeded speech and speech compressed by the sampling method. ... Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility.

claims.claim: The intelligibility threshold for time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners is approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second, setting a perceptual upper bound for accelerated speech comprehension.
claims.quote: The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second (Versfeld and Dreschler 2002).

claims.claim: An automated adaptive threshold method can determine the maximum speech rate an individual listener can understand, defining a measurable per-listener intelligibility limit for accelerated speech.
claims.quote: An automated threshold method has been developed for determining the maximum rate of speech understood by individual listeners.

claims.claim: The speech-rate threshold measures intelligibility rather than comprehension, because threshold values correlated only weakly with traditional comprehension test scores.
claims.quote: A second experiment correlated threshold values with traditional comprehension test scores, finding generally low correlations, indicating the method is not a measure of comprehension.

claims.claim: Listeners can identify accelerated speech with near 100% accuracy when it is time-compressed from a normal rate of about 3-4 syllables per second up to roughly 8 syllables per second.
claims.quote: Versfeld and Dreschler found that listeners could identify speech with near 100% accuracy when it was accelerated from a normal rate of 3-4 syllables per second to a time-compressed rate of roughly 8 syllables per second.

claims.claim: Pocket Casts' Trim Silence feature removes silences from audio without altering the cadence (pitch/timing) of the speaker's voice, a form of pause/silence compression.
claims.quote: Trim silences from audio without altering the cadence of the host (sometimes called 'smart silence' or 'silence removal').

claims.claim: Trim Silence offers three discrete aggressiveness levels (Mild, Medium, Mad Max) rather than a continuous parameter, controlling how aggressively silent gaps are compressed.
claims.quote: Users can select from three intensity options—Mild (default), Medium, or Mad Max

claims.claim: Pocket Casts includes a Volume Boost effect that raises playback loudness independently of the device volume, an intelligibility/audibility enhancement for quiet recordings.
claims.quote: Increases the volume of playback without changing your device volume.

claims.claim: Pocket Casts caps playback speed at 3x on mobile apps and 5x on web/desktop, well below the 16x+ speeds referenced in the research question, indicating mainstream podcast apps do not support extreme speed-up.
claims.quote: Web/Desktop apps: Range extends from 0.5x to 5x maximum speed.

claims.claim: The SSDRC method improves speech-in-noise intelligibility by cascading two stages: spectral shaping (frequency-domain enhancement) followed by dynamic range compression (time-domain enhancement), under a constraint that overall signal power is held constant before and after processing (no added loudness).
claims.quote: The approach combines two methods in sequence: "spectral shaping" followed by "dynamic range compression." The system operates under the constraint that overall signal power remains constant before and after processing.

claims.claim: Spectral shaping plus dynamic range compression yields substantial equivalent-SNR intelligibility gains in noise, reported as 4 dB in speech-shaped noise (SSN) and 8 dB in competing-speaker (CS) noise.
claims.quote: The paper reports substantial SNR gains: "improvement of 4 dB (SSN) and 8 dB (CS)."

claims.claim: The method was validated with a formal human listening test and evaluated using the Speech Intelligibility Index (SII) metric, outperforming prior state-of-the-art intelligibility-enhancement methods on that metric.
claims.quote: the authors assert their "non-parametric way to improve the intelligibility of speech in noise" outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of the Speech Intelligibility Index metric

claims.claim: The technique was tested specifically at low signal-to-noise ratios against two distinct masker types: stationary speech-shaped noise and non-stationary competing-speaker noise.
claims.quote: Two noise types were examined at low SNR levels: Speech-shaped noise (SSN) [and] Competing speaker noise (CS)

claims.claim: Some blind individuals can comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates up to about 22 syllables per second, roughly 3x the ~8 syllables/second ceiling of sighted listeners.
claims.quote: repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second

claims.claim: On ultra-fast speech at 16 syllables/second, sighted subjects scored consistently below 16% accuracy while blind subjects averaged about 59.7% (range 0-93%), demonstrating a large blind-vs-sighted gap at extreme speeds.
claims.quote: sighted performance on ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech "consistently fell below 16%" accuracy, versus blind subjects ranging from 0-93% (mean 59.7%)

claims.claim: The ultra-fast comprehension ability in blind listeners did not significantly correlate with onset of vision loss or duration of blindness, indicating the skill is not simply explained by how early or how long someone has been blind.
claims.quote: The enhanced perceptual skills of blind listeners did not show a significant correlation with either the onset of vision loss (r = -.347, p = .224) or with the duration of blindness

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages visual brain regions, specifically right primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, indicating cross-modal recruitment of visual cortex for accelerated auditory processing.
claims.quote: Key activated regions included: "right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1)", "contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG)", "bilateral pulvinar (Pv)"

claims.claim: The accelerated speech stimuli were created by time-compressing formant-synthesized text-to-speech utterances using Praat from a normal rate of 4-6 syl/s to 8 syl/s (moderately fast) and 16 syl/s (ultra-fast).
claims.quote: Researchers used "speech processing software Praat" to compress sentences from normal rate (4-6 syl/s) to moderately fast (8 syl/s) and ultra-fast (16 syl/s) rates. Stimuli were "formant synthesized" using text-to-speech technology.

claims.claim: Single Frequency Filtering (SFF) controls both spectral and temporal resolution via a single filter parameter (the pole location on the negative real axis relative to the unit circle in the z-plane), and the SFF magnitude (envelope) plus phase across frequencies can resynthesize the original speech signal.
claims.quote: In the SFF method, the spectral and temporal resolutions can be controlled using a single parameter of the filter... The SFF magnitude (envelope) and phase at several frequencies can be used to synthesize the original speech signal.

claims.claim: Speech is more intelligible when it has a higher local dynamic range of amplitude (fine structure) and/or a lower global dynamic range of amplitude (gross structure) in both the spectral and temporal domains.
claims.quote: the speech signal is more intelligible when it has higher dynamic range of amplitude locally (fine structure) and/or lower dynamic range of amplitude globally (gross structure) in both the spectral and temporal domains.

claims.claim: The method enhances intelligibility by modifying the spectral fine structure to produce sharper and sustained harmonics, and modifying the spectral gross structure to resemble the average gross spectral characteristics of more intelligible speech.

claims.quote: auditory timing information is derived from the acoustic signal at a pre-cortical stage, presumably, at the level of the SC, and then transferred to visual cortex via pulvinar

claims.claim: The source is the seminal peer-reviewed review article 'Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech' by E. Foulke and T. G. Sticht, published in Psychological Bulletin, 1969, vol. 72(1), pp. 50-62 (DOI 10.1037/h0027575, PMID 4897155).
claims.quote: Authors: E Foulke, T G Sticht; Title: Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech; Journal: Psychological Bulletin; Year: 1969; Volume/Issue: 72(1); Pages: 50-62; PMID: 4897155; DOI: 10.1037/h0027575

claims.claim: The review establishes that comprehension of accelerated (time-compressed) speech begins to decline at approximately 275 words per minute, a threshold widely attributed to this 1969 paper and tied to the processing capacity of the phonological loop.
claims.quote: previous work has indicated that speech comprehension begins to decline at around 275 words per minute (Foulke & Sticht, 1969) ... when information is presented at speeds that surpass what the phonological loop ... can handle (275 words per minute; see Foulke & Sticht, 1969)

claims.claim: The paper reviews methods for generating time-compressed speech and methods for assessing the effect of compression on word intelligibility and listening comprehension of connected discourse, distinguishing intelligibility from comprehension.
claims.quote: Methods for generating time-compressed speech are reviewed, along with methods for assessing the effect of compression on word intelligibility and listening comprehension, including experiments on word intelligibility and comprehensibility of connected discourse.

claims.claim: Time-compressed/accelerated speech is defined as speech reproduced in less than its original production time, useful when listeners must rely on hearing to obtain language information.
claims.quote: Time-compressed or accelerated speech is speech which has been reproduced in less than the original production time and may prove useful in situations where people must rely on listening to obtain language information.

claims.claim: Combining spectral modification (SSDRC) with temporal modification (GCRetime retiming) into a spectro-temporal approach reduced keyword errors substantially more than either technique alone, yielding a near three-fold reduction in error rate versus unmodified speech.
claims.quote: Errors in the spectro-temporal condition were substantially lower than following spectral or temporal modification alone, with a three-fold reduction compared to unmodified speech.

claims.claim: At the adverse SNR, spectral modification alone gave ~27 percentage-point gain and temporal modification ~21 p.p., but the spectro-temporal combination gave a 41 p.p. gain (keyword score 79%, cutting errors from 62% to 21%); the moderate SNR gain was 21 p.p. (error-rate reduction factor 3.3).
claims.quote: spectral modification alone produced a gain of nearly 27 percentage points (p.p.), while both temporal modification techniques led to gains of nearly 21 p.p. The combination of spectral and temporal modifications resulted in a gain of 41 p.p., corresponding to a keyword score of 79%, a near three-fold reduction in error rate over the Plain baseline (62% errors vs. 21% errors). The moderate SNR led to a 21 p.p. gain from spectro-temporal modification, corresponding to an error rate reduction factor of 3.3.

claims.claim: Temporal modification ('retiming') reduces masking not by redistributing energy but by changing the temporal distribution of speech segments (altering segment durations or inserting pauses to shift them relative to a fluctuating masker); GCRetime finds the optimal sequence of local expansions/contractions that maximise glimpses of the target through the masker.
claims.quote: GCRetime finds the optimal sequence of local expansions and contractions of the target speech signal that jointly maximise an objective function in the presence of a fluctuating masker. In GCRetime, the objective function minimises energetic masking, estimated using glimpse proportion

claims.claim: The spectral and temporal mechanisms are not independent: combined gains fell short of the independence prediction (predicted 86% vs actual 79% at adverse SNR; predicted 97% vs actual 91% at moderate SNR), indicating overlap/redundancy rather than fully additive benefit.
claims.quote: This leads to predictions of 86% for the adverse condition (actual: 79%) and 97% at the moderate SNR (actual: 91%).

claims.claim: Intelligibility across all conditions was very well predicted (rho=.96) by a glimpse-based energetic-masking metric (GPext) that explicitly weights glimpse proportion by the modified-to-unmodified speech duration ratio, implying speech-rate/timing changes are captured by peripheral energetic-masking accounts.
claims.quote: For the conditions of the current experiment, GPext is highly-correlated with intelligibility [ρ = .96, p < .001] ... GPext takes speech rate changes into account by weighting glimpse proportion by a factor corresponding to the ratio of the modified speech duration to the unmodified speech duration.

claims.quote: At first, it requires conscious effort to understand. With years of practice, comprehension becomes automatic. This is just like learning a new language.

claims.quote: formant synthesis is consistent. Since every sound is generated using the same mathematical model, pronunciation is always identical. This predictability makes it possible to understand speech at higher rates.

claims.claim: Natural-sounding TTS voices are harder to understand at high speeds because their human-mimicking variations in timing, pitch, and emphasis degrade intelligibility when accelerated.
claims.quote: variations in timing, pitch, and emphasis that mimic human speech...makes them much harder to understand at higher speeds

claims.quote: For routine tasks like reading emails, documentation, or familiar code patterns, 800 WPM works perfectly

claims.claim: WSOLA (Waveform-Similarity Overlap-Add) improves on basic OLA by identifying similar waveform regions to optimize frame alignment, yielding better audio quality during time-scale modification.
claims.quote: An advancement over basic OLA that intelligently identifies similar waveform regions to optimize frame alignment, improving audio quality during modification.

claims.claim: TD-PSOLA enables both time-stretching and pitch-shifting but requires external pitch tracking data to operate.

claims.claim: The Phase Vocoder is a frequency-domain TSM approach (with a phase-locking variant) that supports non-linear/flexible time-stretching via 2xN arrays of anchor points mapping input sample points.
claims.quote: Frequency-domain approach with phase locking variant. Described as supporting flexible stretching through "2 x n array of anchor points which contains the sample points of the input signal in the first row."

claims.claim: PyTSMod implements four distinct TSM algorithms (OLA, WSOLA, TD-PSOLA, and Phase Vocoder), and its implementations are based on peer-reviewed sources including Driedger & Muller's review of time-scale modification of music signals and Zolzer's DAFX.
claims.quote: Implementation based on peer-reviewed sources: Driedger & Müller (2014) ... Driedger & Müller (2016): "A review of time-scale modification of music signals" ... Zölzer (2011): DAFX: Digital Audio Effects

claims.claim: PyTSMod supports non-linear (variable) time-stretching, where the stretch factor changes across the signal via anchor point arrays, rather than only a single constant speed factor.
claims.quote: Non-linear TSM: Supports variable time-stretching via anchor point arrays

claims.quote: Changes the sound to play at faster or slower tempo than originally without affecting the sound pitch.

claims.quote: Changes the sound pitch or key while keeping the original tempo (speed).

claims.quote: GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL) v2.1

claims.claim: Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of changing an audio signal's playback speed (speeding up or slowing down) without changing its pitch, which is the foundational operation behind speed-listening playback.
claims.quote: Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of speeding up or slowing down an audio signal's playback speed without changing its pitch.

claims.claim: Classical TSM methods WSOLA and the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) preserve perceptual quality of harmonic signals well but introduce noticeable artifacts on percussive signals; no single method handles all signal types, so combining approaches reduces artifacts.
claims.quote: classical TSM procedures based on waveform similarity overlap-add (WSOLA) [4] or on the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) [5–7] are capable of preserving the perceptual quality of harmonic signals to a high degree, but introduce noticeable artifacts when modifying percussive signals. However, it is possible to substantially reduce artifacts by combining different TSM approaches.

claims.claim: WSOLA improves comprehensibility over plain OLA by allowing small shifts (up to plus or minus delta_max samples) of analysis-frame positions, choosing the shift that maximizes cross-correlation so periodic (harmonic) structures align in overlapping regions and phase-jump artifacts are reduced.
claims.quote: This method's core idea is to allow for slight shifts (of up to ±∆max ∈ Z samples) of the analysis frames' positions... The main idea is to adjust successive synthesis frames in such a way that periodic structures in the frames' waveforms are aligned in the overlapping regions.

claims.claim: WSOLA-style time-domain methods suffer transient doubling/stuttering when stretching (alpha greater than 1) and transient skipping when compressing (alpha less than 1), which is especially audible on percussive/strong-onset material like drums and piano.
claims.quote: A prominent problem with WSOLA-like methods is known as transient doubling or stuttering... While transient doubling commonly occurs when stretching a signal (α > 1), transient skipping usually happens when the signal is compressed (α < 1). These artifacts are particularly noticeable when modifying signals with percussive components, such as recordings of instruments with strong onsets (e.g., drums, piano).

claims.claim: The phase vocoder preserves horizontal phase coherence (no phase-jump artifacts) but destroys vertical phase coherence, smearing transients and producing 'phasiness' (reverberant, 'less bite', 'loss of presence'); identity phase locking (Laroche & Dolson) reduces phasiness and transient smearing.
claims.quote: the vertical phase coherence... is usually destroyed in the phase propagation process... transients are often smeared in signals modified with PV-TSM... output signals of PV-TSM commonly have a very distinct sound coloration known as phasiness... This technique, also known as identity phase locking, leads to reduced phasiness artifacts and less smearing of transients.

claims.claim: Rubber Band is an audio library that changes tempo (time-scale) and pitch of audio independently of one another, which is the core time-scale modification capability relevant to making sped-up speech intelligible.
claims.quote: a library and utility program that permits changing the tempo and pitch of an audio recording independently of one another

claims.claim: Rubber Band offers two distinct time-stretching engines: R2 (Faster), the traditional default available since v2.x, and R3 (Finer), a newer higher-quality engine, representing a quality-versus-CPU tradeoff in time-scale modification.
claims.quote: The R3 engine produces higher-quality results than R2 for most material, especially complex mixes, vocals and other sounds that have soft onsets and smooth pitch changes, and music with substantial bass content. However, it uses much more CPU than the R2 engine.

claims.claim: The R2 engine exposes a tunable 'crispness' parameter (values 0-6) that adjusts how transients/onsets are handled during stretching, a control directly relevant to preserving intelligibility of stretched audio.
claims.quote: Crispness selection: -c flag with numerical values 0-6, noted as particularly relevant for the R2 engine

claims.claim: Rubber Band performs dynamic time-stretching, computing per-region stretch ratios and processing decisions rather than a single fixed ratio, and exposes these via a Vamp inspection plugin.
claims.quote: a Vamp plugin for inspecting "dynamic stretch ratios and other decisions taken by the Rubber Band Library"

claims.claim: Rubber Band is an actively maintained, frequency-domain time-stretching/pitch-shifting library, with version 4.0.0 released October 25, 2024 and copyright spanning 2007-2024.
claims.quote: The most recent version mentioned is v4.0.0, released October 25, 2024.

claims.claim: Enhancing the temporal modulations of speech (especially low-frequency temporal modulations of the envelope) significantly increases word recognition / intelligibility in noise, because temporal modulations are a key aspect of speech perception and intelligibility. This grounds 'prosody/envelope processing' as a comprehensibility technique.
claims.quote: DMOD ... enhances the temporal modulations of speech which are a key aspect of speech perception [19],[20],[21] and intelligibility [22], and hence significantly increases word recognition in noise.

claims.claim: Intelligibility can be enhanced by reallocating energy from louder to quieter parts of speech (in both frequency and time domains) using a power-law compression function A'=A^alpha (0<alpha<1), which boosts low-energy regions relative to high-energy ones without simply amplifying the whole signal.
claims.quote: DMOD uses a mathematical equation to increase the energy of quieter parts compared to louder parts of speech; A0k [t] = Ak (t)α , 0 < α < 1 is a non-linear function ... This technique increases the low-frequency temporal modulations of speech and thus significantly improves intelligibility.

claims.claim: The RMOD intelligibility-enhancement algorithm operates in real time with approximately 2 ms delay / 2 ms frame length, making it independent of pitch period and speaker gender, which is required for low-latency real-time applications (telephony, hearing aids).
claims.quote: RMOD ... performs real-time energy reallocation (approx 2ms delay) ... Since it is designed to work on 2ms frame length, it is also independent of the pitch period and hence the speaker’s gender unlike other techniques [16, 18].

claims.claim: RMOD achieves objective intelligibility gains close to SSDRC (the most successful algorithm in the 2013 Hurricane Challenge) while scoring equal or higher on distortion/quality-aware measures, and subjective tests with 22 listeners show RMOD preserves quality (preference similar to unmodified speech) whereas SSDRC gets negative quality scores.
claims.quote: Objective evaluations of intelligibility show that RMOD produces intelligibility enhancements that are close to the levels of SSDRC. RMOD has equivalent or higher scores than SSDRC when using objective measures that also account for speech distortions and quality. Subjective evaluations of quality verify that RMOD maintains speech quality and is preferred over SSDRC.

claims.claim: A scaling operation that smoothly bounds very low energies (rather than hard thresholding) prevents excessive amplification of quiet regions and avoids audible distortion at boundary decisions, preserving the overall waveform shape during intelligibility enhancement.
claims.quote: This scaling operation prevents any very low energies from being excessively amplified which could otherwise lead to audible distortions ... the threshold operation (threshold value 0.015) leads to a distortion at the boundary decision around the 110th frequency bin.

result.evidence: Verbatim quote confirmed by extracting text directly from the primary source PDF (Driedger & Müller 2016, "A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals", Applied Sciences/MDPI 6(2):57). Page 2, lines 58-62 read: "classical TSM procedures based on waveform similarity overlap-add (WSOLA) [4] or on the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) [5–7] are capable of preserving the perceptual quality of harmonic signals to a high degree, but introduce noticeable artifacts when modifying percussive signals. However, it is possible to substantially reduce artifacts by combining different TSM approaches." The claim is an accurate paraphrase, not an overreach. The "no single method handles all signal types" clause is a faithful gloss of the source's adjacent statement that "Retaining these contrasting characteristics usually requires conceptually different TSM approaches." Independent corroboration: SELEBI (arXiv 2602.16421) and "Phase Vocoder Done Right" (arXiv 2202.07382) confirm phase-vocoder "percussion/transient smearing" as a well-known artifact, and the HPSS-based hybrid (OLA for percussive + PV for harmonic) as the standard mitigation — this is DSP-literature consensus, not disputed. Source is a peer-reviewed, highly-cited survey by the leading authority (Müller, AudioLabs Erlangen); quality matches claim strength. 2016 but fundamentals are stable and reaffirmed by 2026 work. Not marketing. No grounds to refute.

VERIFIED against the cited primary source itself (Driedger & Müller, "A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals," Applied Sciences 6(2):57, 2016), extracted verbatim via pdftotext from the saved PDF. Every technical element of the claim matches the paper exactly:

(1) Shifts of analysis-frame positions within ±delta_max: "This method's core idea is to allow for slight shifts (of up to ±∆max ∈ Z samples) of the analysis frames' positions" and "the position of the analysis frame xm was shifted by ∆m ∈ [−∆max : ∆max] samples."

(2) Choosing the shift that maximizes cross-correlation: the paper defines cross-correlation metric c(q,p,∆)=Σ q(r)p(r+∆) (Eq. 9) and states the chosen shift is "∆m+1 that maximizes the cross-correlation of x̃m and x⁺m+1 by ∆m+1 = argmax(...)".

(3) Aligning periodic/harmonic structures in overlapping regions: "The main idea is to adjust successive synthesis frames in such a way that periodic structures in the frames' waveforms are aligned in the overlapping regions" and "periodic structures of the adjusted analysis frame are optimally aligned with structures of the previously copied synthesis frame ym in the overlapping region." The paper also confirms local periodicities correspond to harmonic sounds.

(4) Reduced phase-jump artifacts vs OLA: OLA distortions "are also known as phase jump artifacts"; WSOLA is "One time-domain strategy to reduce phase jump artifacts as caused by OLA," and "WSOLA can improve on some of the drawbacks of OLA."

Adversarial checks all fail to refute: (a) Source is the exact peer-reviewed primary review paper, highest quality for this claim. (b) Independent WebSearch corroborates identically (UMU lecture notes, PracticeSession, Springer, GitHub TSM repo) with no contradicting source. (c) WSOLA fundamentals (Verhelst & Roelands 1993) are textbook-stable, not outdated/fast-moving. (d) Not marketing/forum speculation. The only minor liberality is the word "comprehensibility" where the paper says perceptual "quality"/harmonic preservation, but this does not misrepresent the mechanism and is not an overreach worth a refutation.

The supporting quote is verbatim from the primary source — Driedger & Müller (2016), "A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals," Applied Sciences 6(2):57 (MDPI, peer-reviewed): "Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of speeding up or slowing down an audio signal's playback speed without changing its pitch." The "foundational operation behind speed-listening" framing (not a direct quote but the claim-author's gloss) is also accurate: Wikipedia explicitly connects TSM/time-compressed speech to "speed hearing" and "Listen to podcasts in half the time," and multiple application sources describe pitch-preserved speed-up of podcasts/audiobooks as "speed listening." Source quality (peer-reviewed survey by recognized DSP researcher Meinard Müller) matches the claim's strength; the definition is stable and not outdated; not marketing. Could not find any source disputing or heavily qualifying the definition.

Source quality matches claim strength; definition is foundational and not time-sensitive/outdated; not marketing or forum speculation. Attempted refutation failed on all five checklist points.

1) QUOTE SUPPORTS CLAIM (not an overreach). The primary source is Buus & Florentine / Heimstra-style 1977 paper "A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech" (Perception & Psychophysics, DOI 10.3758/BF03199702). The abstract (confirmed verbatim via the publisher PDF metadata at link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.3758/BF03199702.pdf) defines the threshold as "the maximum rate of speech understood by individual listeners," and Experiment 1 "compared thresholds of two types of rapid speech reportedly different in intelligibility: simple speeded speech and speech compressed by the sampling method... Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility." A HIGHER threshold = a FASTER maximum understandable speech rate. So sampling-compressed speech remained intelligible at faster rates than naive speeded speech. The claim's interpretation ("sampling-based compression preserves intelligibility at faster rates than naive speed-up") is the correct reading of "higher threshold," not a misread.

2) NO CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE FOUND; corroborated by independent sources. The mechanism is well-established and uncontested: simple "speeded" playback uniformly accelerates the waveform, raising pitch/F0 ("chipmunk" effect) and degrading intelligibility, whereas the sampling method periodically discards short segments to shorten duration while preserving fundamental frequency and spectral/prosodic structure (corroborated by the "Techniques, Perception, and Applications of Time-Compressed Speech" survey, ResearchGate; Grokipedia "Time-compressed speech"; and the general TSM literature). This is the foundational, decades-stable result underlying modern OLA/SOLA/WSOLA pitch-preserving time-scale modification, so it is not "outdated" in the sense of being superseded — later methods build on, rather than contradict, it.

3) SOURCE QUALITY MATCHES CLAIM STRENGTH. Peer-reviewed primary journal article (Perception & Psychophysics, a Psychonomic Society journal); also indexed as DTIC report ADA061172. Not marketing, not a press release, not forum speculation. The claim is a modest, mechanistic finding well within what the source's own statistically-significant result states.

CAVEATS (qualifications, not refutations): (a) The paper's Experiment 2 found threshold values correlate only weakly/low with traditional comprehension test scores — the authors note the method "is not a measure of comprehension" but of intelligibility. The claim correctly scopes itself to "intelligibility," so this caveat does not undermine it; it actually aligns. (b) The study is from 1977 and uses one specific older sampling-compression implementation; modern pitch-synchronous methods (PSOLA/WSOLA) further improve on the basic sampling method, but that strengthens rather than contradicts the directional claim. Given strong quote support, primary peer-reviewed source, and broad corroboration, refuted=false.

The empirical core of the claim is robustly supported. Multiple independent sources confirm that Foulke & Sticht (1969), a seminal review in Psychological Bulletin, concluded that listening comprehension "declines at a slow rate as word rate is increased, until a rate of approximately 275 wpm is reached, and at a faster rate thereafter." The 275 wpm threshold is genuinely and widely attributed to this 1969 paper across the literature (e.g., a 2021 UCLA lecture-speed study and a 2024 Educational Psychology Review article both cite it). The supporting quote is verbatim from a real peer-reviewed source: the 2024 paper "The Effect of Playback Speed and Distractions on the Comprehension of Audio and Audio-Visual Materials" (Educational Psychology Review) states "increasing playback speed can have negative effects... when information is presented at speeds that surpass what the phonological loop, which channels auditory information, can handle (275 words per minute; see Foulke & Sticht, 1969)." CAVEAT/WEAKNESS: The "phonological loop" framing is anachronistic relative to the 1969 source — Baddeley & Hitch introduced the phonological loop in 1974, five years AFTER Foulke & Sticht (1969). So the 1969 paper itself did not tie the threshold to the phonological loop; that linkage is a modern (post-1974) theoretical reinterpretation layered onto the empirical figure by the citing review. However, the claim does not assert the 1969 paper made this link — it merely says the threshold is "tied to" the phonological loop, which faithfully paraphrases how the citing source frames it. Because the empirical threshold is well-established and the claim accurately reports its source, it does not meet the refutation bar (unsupported/contradicted/low-quality/outdated/marketing).

The quote is genuine — the Frontiers 2013 paper (Dietrich et al.) does state "the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)." HOWEVER the claim is refuted on three grounds: (1) NOT A PRIMARY MEASUREMENT — the 8 syl/s figure is cited secondhand from Moos & Trouvain (2007), a conference proceedings paper (Interspeech), referenced as citation #28; the Frontiers paper itself is an fMRI study of BLIND listeners and does not measure the sighted ceiling. (2) CONTRADICTED BY PRIMARY LITERATURE — peer-reviewed time-compressed-speech studies place the untrained normal-hearing limit much higher: the 50% intelligibility threshold for young normal-hearing listeners "ranges from 11 to 16 syllables per second" (Scientific Reports / PMC9938863, nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x), with ~99.8% intelligibility at 6.6 sps and still ~42% at 15.6 sps; comprehension "begins declining noticeably above the 11-16 sps range" and speech becomes unintelligible only around 15 sps. This is roughly 1.5x-2x the claimed 8 syl/s. The Frontiers authors' own prior work also noted sighted subjects still scored (below 20% but nonzero) at 16 syl/s, not a hard cutoff at 8. (3) OVERREACH — "establishing the perceptual ceiling for non-experts" overstates what is really a high-accuracy comfortable upper bound; the true perceptual ceiling per the broader literature is ~12-16 syl/s. The 8 syl/s number is a comparative reference point in an ultra-fast-speech training paradigm, not an established perceptual ceiling.
