Source quality (multiple peer-reviewed neuroscience journals, fMRI/MEG studies) matches the claim strength.

The quote "ca. 8 syl/s" is accurately lifted from the Frontiers paper (Dietrich et al. 2013, abstract/intro: "exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"), but the CLAIM overreaches by reframing this as "the perceptual ceiling for non-experts." Three problems: (1) The 8 syl/s figure is cited from Moos & Trouvain (2007), where 4-8 syl/s is repeatedly described as the NORMAL/conversational speaking rate, not a comprehension ceiling ("normal speaking rate amounts to only 4-8 syllables/s"). The Frontiers paper itself uses 8 syl/s as its "moderately fast" experimental stimulus condition, not its intelligibility limit. (2) DIRECT CONTRADICTION from peer-reviewed time-compressed-speech literature: young normal-hearing UNTRAINED listeners reach 50% intelligibility (speech reception threshold, SRTrate) at 11-16+ syllables/s, with median thresholds ~12.3-16.7 syl/s (PMC9085996, "The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech..."), and ~15 syl/s is the rate at which speech becomes unintelligible. That is roughly DOUBLE the claimed 8 syl/s ceiling. (3) The paper's loose phrasing "maximum performance level" is an interpretive characterization of a single conference-paper figure, not an established perceptual limit; WebFetch of the primary source confirms it is "the typical upper boundary... not a hard neurological ceiling," used to contrast trained blind subjects (22 syl/s) against controls. The claim therefore misrepresents a normal-speech-rate / study-specific contrast value as a perceptual comprehension ceiling, and is contradicted by stronger time-compressed-speech intelligibility data.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/ (Time-compressed speech: untrained normal-hearing 50% intelligibility thresholds ~11-16.7 syl/s); Moos & Trouvain 2007 (16th ICPhS) where 4-8 syl/s = normal speaking rate, not a comprehension ceiling

result.evidence: The claim is an exact, verbatim match to the primary source (Dietrich, Hertrich, Ackermann 2013, "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar," BMC Neuroscience 14:74, PMC3847124). The source states for 16 syl/s ultra-fast speech: blind listeners "extended ... from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)" and sighted "performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)." All four reported figures (below 16% sighted; 59.7% mean, 0-93% range blind) reproduce exactly. A second independent search (Frontiers/BMC corpus) corroborates the same gap (blind up to >90%, sighted below ~20%) and the same research group/methodology. Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed primary fMRI study. One fair qualification (not a refutation of the numbers): the blind cohort were specifically TRAINED/proficient ultra-fast-speech listeners (radio/screen-reader users), not blind people in general, and "comprehension" here = % correctly reproduced items in a sentence-repetition task. The claim's wording ("blind subjects averaged about 59.7%") is accurate to what the paper reports for its sample. No credible source contradicts the figures. Study is from 2013 but this is a stable perceptual/neuroscience finding, not a fast-moving benchmark, so age is not disqualifying.

Primary source (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, BMC Neuroscience 2013; PMC3847124, "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar") fully supports the claim verbatim. At 16 syl/s ultra-fast speech: SIGHTED subjects' "performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD +/-4.7%)"; BLIND subjects ranged "from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD +/-24.0%)". Groups significantly differed (two-sample t-test, T = 7.74, p < .001). Sample: 14 blind, 12 sighted subjects. The claim's quote correctly attributes "consistently fell below 16%" to sighted and 0-93%/mean 59.7% to blind. The large blind-vs-sighted gap at extreme speed is exactly what the study reports. Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed neuroscience study with multiple follow-up publications from the same group (Frontiers 2013, PLOS One 2015). Note: the claim correctly uses "16 syllables/second" (the study's actual metric), not the "16x playback" framing in the research question header. No contradicting evidence found.

The claim is accurately supported by the primary source (BMC Neuroscience 2013, PMC3847124), which states verbatim: "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second" and gives the sighted ceiling as "the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)." The "roughly 3x" is arithmetically sound (22/8 = 2.75). The claim's hedges ("Some blind individuals," "up to about 22") correctly mirror the source's hedges ("may enable," "up to"), accurately capturing that 22 syl/s is an exceptional upper-limit value achieved by highly-trained individuals, not a typical average. Provenance: the 22 syl/s figure is cited from prior peer-reviewed work (Moos & Trouvain, 16th Int'l Congress of Phonetic Sciences, ref [17]); the PMC study itself tested blind subjects at 16 syl/s ultra-fast vs 8 syl/s moderate. Independent corroboration found: Scientific American (up to 25 syl/s blind vs ~10 sighted) and Frontiers in Human Neuroscience report the same phenomenon — these figures bracket the claim rather than contradict it. No retraction, dispute, or credible refutation located. Source quality (peer-reviewed neuroscience) matches the claim strength; not marketing or forum speculation. Only minor nuance (a citation, an upper bound for some individuals) is already reflected in the claim's wording, so it does not warrant refutation.
result.counterSource: https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/why-can-some-blind-people-process/ (cites up to 25 syl/s for blind vs ~10 for sighted — different exact numbers but corroborates, does not refute)

result.evidence: The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source and corroborated by multiple independent peer-reviewed papers. The PMC3745084 article (Hertrich, Dietrich, Ackermann, "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?", Frontiers in Psychology, 2013) states verbatim: "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s." Independent corroboration: BMC Neuroscience 2013 (PMC3847124) states blind subjects "understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second" vs. "maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)," with measured comprehension of 59.7% mean for blind listeners vs 9.0% for sighted controls on 16 syl/s material. A separate Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013 paper (PMC3805979) documents training-induced (6 months) acquisition of 18 syl/s comprehension in late-blind participants. The normal-rate figure is consistently cited as 4-8 syl/s (one paper says ~6). Source quality (peer-reviewed neuroscience journals, primary empirical fMRI/behavioral studies) matches the claim's strength. CAVEAT/SCOPE NOTE: The claim correctly states "16 syllables per second," NOT "16x playback speed" — these are different quantities. The research question conflates them ("playback speeds greater than 16x"), but the CLAIM itself is accurate about rate in syllables/s. The claim is also narrowly about trained/proficient BLIND subjects, not the general population — which the claim correctly scopes.

The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source. WebFetch of PMC3745084 (Hertrich, Dietrich, Ackermann, "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?", Frontiers in Psychology, 2013) confirms the exact abstract sentence: "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s." Multiple independent peer-reviewed corroborations exist: BMC Neuroscience 2013 (PMC3847124) and Frontiers Human Neuroscience 2013 fMRI/training studies show trained blind subjects reach >90% comprehension at 16 syll/s while sighted controls fall below 20%; Scientific American and Neuropsychologia 2018 (ScienceDirect S0028393218300897) report the same phenomenon, with some sources citing up to 22-25 syll/s. The parenthetical "normal speaking rate 4-8 syllables/s" the claim adds is also consistent with the literature (Wikipedia Speech tempo: ~4 syll/s English, natural range 2.5-8 syll/s; one source notes speech becomes unintelligible for untrained listeners beyond ~15 syll/s — which is exactly what makes the trained-blind ability notable, reinforcing the claim). No credible source disputes or qualifies it away. Quote is accurate, in-context, from a primary peer-reviewed source by an established research group; claim strength matches source quality. Could not refute.

Primary peer-reviewed source PMC3847124 (Dietrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013) states verbatim in its Behavioral Results: "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech utterances (16 syl/s)... extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)" and "In sighted individuals, performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)." This matches the claim's "range 0-93%," "about 59.7%," and "consistently below 16%" word-for-word. The blind-vs-sighted gap is statistically significant (two-sample t-test T=7.74, p<.001). The phrase "scored consistently below 16%" uses the paper's exact language; the true sighted mean was actually 9.0%, so the 16% figure is the documented ceiling, not an overreach. Source is a primary fMRI study, appropriate for the claim's strength. Corroborated by Scientific American coverage and the companion Frontiers training study (PMC3805979); no contradicting source found. Cannot refute.

Claim is well-supported and quoted verbatim from the primary source (Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann, "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?", Frontiers in Psychology, 2013, PMC3745084): "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s." Although that article is a theory/review paper, the claim is independently corroborated by peer-reviewed EMPIRICAL research: the BMC Neuroscience fMRI study (Dietrich et al. 2013, PMC3847124, n=14 blind + 12 sighted, ethics-approved) states blind people "understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables per second," used 16 syl/s ultra-fast test materials, and cites normal rate of "4-6 syl/s." Trained blind subjects achieved >90% comprehension at 16 syl/s vs <20% for sighted controls. Further independent corroboration: Scientific American, a 2018 Neuropsychologia study (ScienceDirect S0028393218300897), and Frontiers training-reorganization studies. No contradicting source found. Minor nuance: the claim's parenthetical "4-8 syllables/s" differs slightly from this article's stated "6 syllables/s" / empirical "4-6 syl/s" (other sources do cite up to 8 syl/s for max sighted performance), but this does not affect the core claim's validity.

The claim is a faithful, accurate paraphrase of the source, not an overreach. The supporting quote is verbatim from Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann (2013), "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?" (Frontiers in Psychology; PMC3745084): "the 'bottleneck' in sighted subjects for the comprehension of time-compressed speech arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints of the extraction of acoustic/phonetic features." The "frontal brain structures" component is also directly supported — the article attributes the buffer mechanism to "left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG), anterior insula, precentral cortex, and upper frontal cortex including the supplementary motor area" and states the constraint "is, presumably, linked to frontal brain structures." The mechanism (phonological info "overwritten before fully encoded," backward masking) is explicit in the text.

Attempted refutations: (1) Source type — it is a peer-reviewed "Hypothesis and Theory" article, not original empirical data, and uses the hedge "presumably." But the original claim is itself appropriately hedged ("is linked to"), and the same Tübingen group's empirical fMRI/MEG papers (BMC Neuroscience 2013, PMC3847124; Frontiers Hum Neurosci 2013, PMC3805979) provide supporting evidence (blind listeners' resistance to backward masking tied to visual-cortex activity). (2) Contradicting literature — searches found no source disputing the phonological-storage interpretation. Complementary work adds auditory-motor synchronization and linguistic prediction as further adaptive factors, but these supplement rather than contradict the buffering thesis; auditory working memory is independently shown to predict speech recognition under adverse/compressed conditions. (3) Age — 2013, but the topic is specialized and stable, with no newer work overturning it.

The claim's strength matches the (appropriately hedged) source claim, it is current enough, and it is not contradicted, marketing, or forum speculation. Cannot meet the bar for refutation.

Claim is well-supported and traceable to a strong primary source. The supporting quote appears verbatim in PMC3745084 (Hertrich et al., Frontiers in Psychology, 2013), which states the bottleneck "arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints" and that it is "presumably, linked to frontal brain structures" (left IFG, anterior insula, precentral, upper frontal cortex). Crucially, this conclusion originates from the primary empirical fMRI study it cites: Vagharchakian, Dehaene-Lambertz, Pallier & Dehaene (2012), J. Neurosci. 32(26):9089, "A Temporal Bottleneck in the Language Comprehension Network." I verified that primary source directly: it concludes "the main factor is not a sensory limitation but a central amodal processing bottleneck that leads to the overflow of an internal buffer," with buffer regions in left IFG/anterior insula, precentral cortex, and mesial frontal cortex showing inverted-U activation peaking at intermediate compression. Contradiction search found no credible dispute; complementary working-memory-capacity literature and the "now-or-never bottleneck" framework are consistent. Minor caveat: the claim asserts as settled fact what the review source hedges ("presumably," "seems related to"), but the underlying J. Neurosci. study states the buffer-overflow conclusion firmly. This is a slight strength-mismatch, not a refutation. Source quality (high-impact peer-reviewed primary fMRI study) matches the claim's strength.
result.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Searched working-memory/acoustic-processing critiques (e.g., arxiv.org/pdf/2407.06596, PMC9975658 on motor-rhythm-based comprehension); these are complementary, not contradictory. The closest nuance is that acoustic/temporal factors still contribute, but the source explicitly frames buffering as dominant over acoustic-feature-extraction speed.

result.evidence: The claim is accurately supported by the cited source AND its underlying primary literature. The quote is verbatim from Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann (2013), "How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?" (Frontiers in Psychology). The same paper explicitly links the buffer to frontal structures: "The buffer mechanism for the comprehension of continuous speech has been attributed to left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG), anterior insula, precentral cortex, and upper frontal cortex including the supplementary motor area (SMA and pre-SMA)." The underlying PRIMARY source — Vagharchakian et al. (2012), "A Temporal Bottleneck in the Language Comprehension Network," J Neurosci 32(26):9089 — corroborates the substance: a "large modality-independent left-hemispheric language network, including the inferior frontal gyrus... shows a remarkably time-invariant response," and at fast rates "incoming words must be buffered, and intelligibility vanishes when buffer storage and retrieval operations are saturated." Independent corroboration: inserting silent intervals restores intelligibility to sentences compressed at ~33% (argues against a purely acoustic/speed bottleneck, since silence lets the buffer drain), and pSTS/IFG remain time-invariant down to ~40% compression (post-sensory limit). NUANCE / partial caveat: Vagharchakian frames the bottleneck as BOTH a fixed internal processing speed AND buffer saturation — the "fixed speed" is why buffering becomes necessary, while buffer capacity is what determines comprehension failure. So "storage capacity rather than speed constraints" is a slight simplification of a dual mechanism, but it faithfully captures the failure-mode (it is buffer saturation, not an acoustic speed wall, that kills comprehension). Source quality (peer-reviewed J Neurosci primary fMRI study + Frontiers review) matches the non-extraordinary claim; framework is dated (2012-13) but uncontradicted and still cited in 2022 channel-capacity work. No contradicting source found.
result.counterSource: Vagharchakian et al. (2012), J Neurosci 32(26):9089 (PMC6622343) frames the bottleneck as fixed processing speed PLUS buffer saturation, slightly qualifying the claim's "storage capacity rather than speed constraints" wording — but this supports rather than refutes the core claim.

result.summary: The foundational operation behind speed-listening is time-scale modification (TSM): changing playback speed without altering pitch. The two classical pitch-preserving algorithms are WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap-Add) and the phase vocoder (PV-TSM); WSOLA improves comprehensibility over plain overlap-add by shifting analysis frames (up to plus or minus delta_max samples) to maximize cross-correlation and align periodic structures, while PSOLA (Pitch Synchronous Overlap-Add) is preferred when one wants to push the speech modulation spectrum to higher modulation frequencies while preserving spectral features. Sampling/TSM-based compression preserves intelligibility at substantially faster rates than naive (pitch-shifting) speed-up, but comprehension still degrades because individually-intelligible words become less intelligible inside the limited time constraints of a compressed message, and because a temporary phonological-storage buffer (not a hard acoustic speed wall) saturates. Regarding extreme speed-listening: the research question's premise of "16x playback" is not what the literature actually measures — researchers measure speech RATE in syllables/second, not playback multiplier. Normal-hearing/sighted listeners hit a perceptual ceiling around 11-16 syllables/second (comprehension begins declining near ~275 words/minute), whereas trained late-blind individuals can reach up to ~22 syllables/second (roughly 3x the ~8 syl/s sighted ceiling), an adaptation acquired through months of daily exposure that produces both a rapid initial phase and a slower, deeper, training-dependent phase of perceptual learning.

findings.claim: Speed-listening rests on time-scale modification (TSM): speeding up (or slowing down) audio playback WITHOUT changing pitch. This is the foundational, pitch-preserving operation that distinguishes intelligible speed-up from naive resampling (which raises pitch).
findings.evidence: Driedger & Muller (peer-reviewed review, Applied Sciences 2016, Int. Audio Laboratories Erlangen): 'Time-scale modification (TSM) is the task of speeding up or slowing down an audio signal's playback speed without changing its pitch.' Corroborated by Stanford CCRMA (J.O. Smith). Multiple sources confirm podcast/audiobook 1.5x-2x speed-up IS TSM, implemented via WSOLA/PSOLA. [merged from claim 0]

findings.claim: WSOLA and the phase vocoder (PV-TSM) preserve perceptual quality of harmonic signals well but introduce audible artifacts on percussive/transient material; no single TSM method handles all signal types, so combining approaches (e.g. harmonic-percussive separation) reduces artifacts. For speech (predominantly harmonic), WSOLA gives very high-quality results.
findings.evidence: Driedger & Muller (Appl. Sci. 2016) verbatim: WSOLA/PV-TSM 'preserv[e] the perceptual quality of harmonic signals to a high degree, but introduce noticeable artifacts when modifying percussive signals... it is possible to substantially reduce artifacts by combining different TSM approaches'; abstract: 'there is no single TSM method that can cope with all kinds of audio signals equally well.' Banai & Lavner (PMC3467231) independently used WSOLA because it 'achieve[s] very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals' and 'maintains acoustic properties like pitch and timbre unchanged.' [merged from claims 1, 10]

findings.claim: WSOLA's comprehensibility advantage over plain OLA comes from allowing small shifts (up to plus/minus delta_max samples) of analysis-frame positions, choosing the shift that maximizes cross-correlation so periodic (harmonic) structures align across overlapping regions, reducing phase-jump artifacts.
findings.evidence: Driedger & Muller Section 4 (verbatim): 'This method's core idea is to allow for slight shifts (of up to +/-delta_max in Z samples) of the analysis frames' positions... compute the optimal shift index that maximizes the cross-correlation... such that periodic structures... are optimally aligned... in the overlapping region.' Original WSOLA paper (Verhelst & Roelands, IEEE 1993) corroborates the waveform-similarity / cross-correlation mechanism. [merged from claim 2]

findings.claim: PSOLA (Pitch Synchronous Overlap-Add) is the alternative TSM choice for uniform time-compression because, as the compression ratio increases, it shifts the speech modulation spectrum toward higher modulation frequencies while preserving the modulation-spectrum shape and spectral features (pitch contour).
findings.evidence: Gransier, van Wieringen & Wouters (J. Assoc. Res. Otolaryngol. 2022, PMC9085996): sentences 'uniformly time-compressed... with the Pitch Synchronous Overlap and Add algorithm (PSOLA)' chosen because it 'shifts the modulation spectrum with increasing compression ratio to higher modulation frequencies... but at the same time preserves the shape of modulation spectrum and the spectral features.' [from claim 8]

findings.claim: Sampling/TSM-based time-compression preserves intelligibility at significantly faster rates than naive speeded (pitch-raising) speed-up: pitch-preserving compression yields a higher speech-rate intelligibility threshold. This is the core empirical justification for using TSM rather than plain fast playback.
findings.evidence: de Haan (1977), Perception & Psychophysics 22:366-372: 'Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility.' A higher threshold = intelligible at faster rate. Sampling method (periodic segment removal, pitch-preserving) outperforms simple speeded (pitch-raising) playback. Foundational (1977) but consistent with current TSM-over-resampling consensus. [from claim 5]

findings.claim: Comprehension degrades under compression in part because words that are individually intelligible become LESS intelligible when packed inside the limited time constraints of a fast message — a processing-time bottleneck. The literature distinguishes word INTELLIGIBILITY from discourse COMPREHENSION, which compression affects differently.
findings.evidence: Heiman et al. (1986), Perception & Psychophysics 40(6):407-411: 'individually compressed words are less intelligible when presented within the limited time constraints of a compressed message.' Foulke & Sticht (1969 review, Psychol. Bull. 72:50-62) established the intelligibility-vs-comprehension distinction, reviewing methods to generate time-compressed speech and assess effects on word intelligibility vs connected-discourse comprehension. [merged from claims 6, 14]

findings.claim: The bottleneck for time-compressed speech in normal/sighted listeners is limited temporary phonological storage capacity (buffer saturation), NOT a hard acoustic speed constraint, and is linked to left frontal structures (inferior frontal gyrus, anterior insula, precentral cortex, SMA/pre-SMA). Inserting silent intervals to let the buffer drain restores intelligibility.
findings.evidence: Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann (Frontiers in Psychology 2013, PMC3745084): the 'bottleneck in sighted subjects... arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints,' attributed to left IFG, anterior insula, precentral cortex, SMA/pre-SMA. Underlying primary source Vagharchakian et al. (2012, J. Neurosci. 32:9089) shows a time-invariant left language network and that intelligibility vanishes when buffer storage/retrieval saturates; silent gaps restore it. Caveat: Vagharchakian frames it as a dual mechanism (fixed internal processing speed forces buffering; buffer capacity determines failure). [from claim 19]

findings.claim: There is a perceptual ceiling for accelerated-speech comprehension in normal-hearing/sighted (untrained) listeners at roughly 11-16 syllables/second; relatedly, comprehension of accelerated speech begins to decline more steeply around ~275 words/minute, a threshold tied (by modern theory) to phonological-loop processing capacity.
findings.evidence: Gransier et al. 2022 (PMC9085996): 'The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second.' Nature Sci. Reports (s41598-023-29755-x): upper limit '11 to 16 syllables per second.' Foulke & Sticht (1969) via 2024 Educ. Psychol. Rev. review: comprehension declines at ~275 wpm, 'speeds that surpass what the phonological loop... can handle.' Caveat: phonological-loop framing is a modern (post-1974 Baddeley) overlay on the 1969 finding. [merged from claims 7, 13]

findings.claim: Trained late-blind/vision-impaired individuals can comprehend ultra-fast (TTS/screen-reader and time-compressed) speech at rates up to ~22 syllables/second — roughly 3x the ~8 syl/s ceiling of sighted listeners and far above the ~4-8 (often cited ~6) syl/s normal speaking rate. This demonstrates the perceptual limit is plastic, not fixed, and shifts with exposure/training.
findings.evidence: Multiple peer-reviewed neuroscience papers from the Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann group converge: blind people 'understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables per second' vs untrained ~8 syl/s (PMC3847124, PMC3805979, Frontiers 2013). Original empirical figure: Moos & Trouvain (2007). At 16 syl/s, sighted accuracy 'consistently fell below 16%' (mean 9.0%) while blind averaged 59.7% (range 0-93%) (PMC3847124). Gransier 2022 notes 'special populations perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second... neural processing... can be altered by exposure and training.' 22/8 = ~2.75x ('roughly 3x'). Caveat: 22 syl/s is the trained upper bound; most experiments tested at 16 syl/s. NOTE: the research question's '16x playback' is a category error — literature measures syllables/second, not playback multiplier. [merged from claims 3, 9, 15, 16, 17, 18]

findings.claim: Adaptation to accelerated speech is real and trainable, with a two-phase structure: a rapid initial adaptation (10-20 sentences improves identification but leaves performance well below ceiling, ~40-85% correct) followed by a slower, deeper, training-dependent phase. Months of daily exposure (e.g. via a TTS screen reader) produce large, lasting gains — four of six trained blind participants reached ~70% word comprehension at 18 syl/s.
findings.evidence: Banai & Lavner (2012, PLoS ONE, PMC3467231, 'Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation'): 'Brief adaptation (10-20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40-85% correct)'; 'Multiday training... results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation,' progressing from a rapid phase to a 'prolonged and more stimulus specific phase' (trained d=1.37 vs control d=0.56). Dietrich et al. (2013, Frontiers Hum. Neurosci., PMC3805979): ~6 months >=1hr/day JAWS+Eloquence TTS training; 'Four of six participants showed... considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)' at 18 syl/s (from <14% pre-training). [merged from claims 4, 11, 12]

result.caveats: PREMISE MISMATCH (most important): The research question asks about playback speeds 'greater than 16x' and 'extreme speed-ups.' The peer-reviewed literature does NOT operate in playback-multiplier units — it measures speech RATE in syllables/second (or words/minute). 16 syllables/second is roughly 2-3x a normal speaking rate (~6-8 syl/s), NOT 16x. There is no credible scientific evidence for intelligible speech comprehension at anything like 16x normal playback; the documented human ceiling is ~22 syl/s (about 3x normal) and only for highly trained blind listeners. Claims of '16x speed listening' in the consumer/forum sphere conflate playback multiplier with rate and were not substantiated by any verified primary source. TIME-SENSITIVITY: TSM signal-processing fundamentals (WSOLA 1993, PSOLA, phase vocoder) and the human-perception/psychophysics findings (1969-2022) are in mature, stable fields; the 1969/1977/1986 foundational papers remain uncontested, so age is not disqualifying. Modern neural/DSP work (2012-2023) updates but does not overturn them. SOURCE STRENGTH: All findings rest on peer-reviewed primary sources (MDPI Applied Sciences review, Springer Perception & Psychophysics, Frontiers, BMC/J. Neurosci., PLoS ONE, J. Assoc. Res. Otolaryngol., Nature Sci. Reports); no marketing/forum/blog sources were used in confirmed claims. POPULATION SCOPE: The most extreme rates (~22 syl/s) are specific to trained late-blind individuals and recruit visual cortex/fusiform/pulvinar via cross-modal plasticity — they do not generalize to the sighted general population. Five claims were refuted in adversarial review (mostly over-precise restatements of the 8 syl/s 'ceiling' and threshold figures), so single-figure 'ceilings' should be read as approximate ranges, not hard constants.
openQuestions: What modern intelligibility-enhancement and prosody/spectral-processing techniques (beyond classical WSOLA/PSOLA TSM) — e.g. dynamic silence/pause compression, adaptive per-phoneme compression, formant/clarity enhancement, or neural-network-based TSM — most improve comprehension at high speed-up factors? The verified corpus covered TSM algorithms and human perceptual limits but did not surface evidence on these specific enhancement methods named in the question.

Does inserting periodic silent gaps to let the phonological buffer drain (shown to restore intelligibility for sighted listeners at high compression) translate into a deployable consumer 'speed-listening' technique, and what gap timing/ratio is optimal?

Can the months-long training-driven adaptation observed in blind listeners be replicated in sighted users with deliberate practice, and how far above the ~11-16 syl/s sighted ceiling can a motivated trained sighted listener realistically reach?

How do non-uniform (content-aware / variable-rate) compression algorithms compare to uniform PSOLA/WSOLA for comprehension at extreme rates, given PSOLA was specifically chosen in one study for its UNIFORM modulation-spectrum behavior?
